findings.title: FolderMonitor, DocumentProcessor, chunkText have no live consumer
findings.code_snippet:
export { FolderMonitor } from './capture/folder-monitor'
export { DocumentProcessor } from './processing/document-processor'
export { chunkText } from './processing/text-chunker'
findings.description: All three new modules are exported from the package barrel but no application or service imports them. The only references are in test files within the same package. No app in apps/ (stage-tamagotchi, stage-web, server) imports FolderMonitor, DocumentProcessor, or chunkText. This is a horizontal-only addition with no live entry point.
findings.suggestion: Wire FolderMonitor and DocumentProcessor into at least one live entry point (e.g., stage-tamagotchi orchestrator service) to form a vertical slice. At minimum, add a TODO-free integration in the Electron main process that watches a user-configured folder and indexes documents on change.

findings.title: No structured logging in FolderMonitor or DocumentProcessor
findings.description: Neither FolderMonitor nor DocumentProcessor use the project's @guiiai/logg structured logging. File watcher lifecycle events (start, stop, error, subscription count) and document extraction operations (file processed, extraction time, failures) are completely invisible in production. The onError callback is the only error path, but watcher start/stop and successful operations produce zero observability.
findings.suggestion: Add `import { useLogg } from '@guiiai/logg'` and log key lifecycle events: watcher start/stop with directory count, file change events at debug level, extraction start/completion with duration_ms, and all error paths with context.

findings.title: DocumentProcessor.extractText has no path validation
findings.description: extractText accepts an arbitrary file path with no validation against path traversal or symlink attacks. If this module is ever exposed via an API or user input (e.g., user picks a folder to watch, and FolderMonitor feeds paths to DocumentProcessor), an attacker could craft symlinks pointing outside the watched directory to read sensitive files like /etc/passwd or credentials. The file path is passed directly to readFile without any containment check.
findings.suggestion: Add a `basePaths` or `allowedRoots` parameter to DocumentProcessor and validate that resolved paths (via path.resolve + fs.realpath) stay within the allowed directory tree before reading. At minimum, resolve symlinks and verify containment.

findings.title: No file size limit before reading entire file into memory
findings.code_snippet: const buffer = await readFile(filePath)
findings.description: extractPdf, extractDocx, and extractPlainText all call readFile() on user-provided file paths with no size check. A multi-gigabyte PDF or a maliciously large file would be read entirely into memory, causing OOM crashes in the Electron process. This applies to all four extraction methods (lines 50, 84, 99, 129).
findings.suggestion: Add a configurable maxFileSizeBytes option (default e.g. 100MB) and stat the file before reading. Reject files exceeding the limit with a descriptive error. For very large text files, consider streaming.

findings.title: DocumentProcessor I/O methods have no try/catch around file reads or dynamic imports
findings.description: extractPdf performs three fallible I/O operations (readFile, dynamic import, getDocument) with no try/catch. A corrupt PDF, missing file, or missing pdfjs-dist dependency will propagate a raw, untyped library error to the caller. The same pattern repeats in extractDocx (line 83), extractXlsx (line 96), and extractPlainText (line 128). All four extraction methods let raw Node.js/library errors escape without wrapping them in a domain-specific error that includes the filePath and operation context. Additionally, three dynamic imports (pdfjs-dist line 52, mammoth line 85, exceljs line 97) are not wrapped in try/catch. If any of these optional dependencies is not installed, the caller gets a raw 'ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND' error with no context about which document type requires which package.
findings.suggestion: Wrap each extraction method body in try/catch and throw a typed error: `throw new Error(`Failed to extract ${documentType} from ${filePath}: ${error.message}`, { cause: error })`. For dynamic imports specifically, provide a helpful error: `const pdfjsLib = await import('pdfjs-dist/legacy/build/pdf.mjs').catch(() => { throw new Error('pdfjs-dist is required for PDF extraction. Install it with: pnpm add pdfjs-dist') })`

findings.title: PDF doc.destroy() not in finally block -- resource leak on error
findings.description: If any page extraction fails (getPage or getTextContent throws), doc.destroy() on line 73 is never called. The PDF document resource leaks. This is the classic try/finally pattern omission. In a long-running process (like a folder monitor that processes many files), leaked PDF document handles will accumulate.
findings.suggestion: Wrap the page extraction loop in try/finally: `const doc = await pdfjsLib.getDocument(...).promise; try { ... } finally { await doc.destroy() }`. This ensures cleanup regardless of whether page extraction succeeds.

findings.title: FolderMonitor.start() continues silently after failed watch subscriptions
findings.description: When watcher.subscribe fails for a directory, the error is forwarded to onError but start() continues and eventually returns normally. If all directories fail, this.subscriptions will be empty, yet isRunning returns false and the caller has no signal that monitoring is completely non-functional. The error is reported via callback but start()'s Promise resolves successfully, so the caller has no way to await and detect total failure.
findings.suggestion: After the loop, check if zero subscriptions succeeded when watchPaths was non-empty: `if (this.subscriptions.length === 0) { throw new Error('Failed to watch any directory') }`. This surfaces total failure to the caller via the returned Promise. Partial failure can remain callback-based.

findings.title: DocumentProcessor: no tests for corrupt/malformed files
findings.description: The error handling suite only tests unsupported file types and non-existent files. It does not test how DocumentProcessor behaves when given corrupt or malformed files for each supported format: a truncated/invalid PDF, a corrupted DOCX (invalid ZIP), a malformed XLSX, or an empty file with a supported extension. These are realistic operational error scenarios that production code will encounter.
findings.suggestion: Add error-path tests for each format: (1) corrupted DOCX (write random bytes to a .docx file), (2) truncated PDF (write partial PDF header), (3) malformed XLSX (write invalid zip to .xlsx), (4) empty file with .txt extension (verify empty string returned, not error). Verify that the errors thrown have meaningful context (file path, format) rather than raw library errors.

findings.title: Integration test excludes FolderMonitor from the pipeline
findings.description: The integration test exercises DocumentProcessor -> chunkText -> VectorStore, but does not include FolderMonitor in the pipeline. FolderMonitor is a key entry point in this feature: it detects file changes and should trigger the document processing pipeline. Without an integration test that wires FolderMonitor.onChange -> DocumentProcessor.extractText -> chunkText -> VectorStore.insert, there is no proof that the full end-to-end pipeline works when a file is created or modified on disk.
findings.suggestion: Add an integration test that: (1) creates a FolderMonitor watching a temp dir, (2) writes a file to that dir, (3) in the onChange callback, runs extractText -> chunkText -> store.insert, (4) verifies chunks appear in the VectorStore after the file event. This proves the full vertical slice from file system event to vector storage.

findings.title: Unsafe `as` casts in PDF text extraction
findings.description: Triple `as` cast chain to extract the str property from pdfjs-dist TextItem. The pdfjs-dist types export TextItem with a str property directly. Using Record<string, unknown> obscures the actual type and bypasses type safety. If the pdfjs-dist API changes, the runtime 'in' check would still pass but the cast could be wrong.
findings.suggestion: Import TextItem from pdfjs-dist types and use a proper type guard: `import type { TextItem } from 'pdfjs-dist/types/src/display/api'`. Then check `if ('str' in item) { return (item as TextItem).str }` or use the hasOwnProperty pattern with a single targeted cast.

findings.title: Unsafe `as` cast on Excel row.values
findings.code_snippet: const cellValues = row.values as (string | number | boolean | null | undefined)[]
findings.description: ExcelJS row.values can contain Date objects, RichText objects, Error objects, and formula results -- not just string|number|boolean|null|undefined. Casting to a narrow union type and then calling String(v) on a Date will produce locale-dependent output (e.g., 'Mon Feb 20 2026 ...' instead of an ISO date). Formula error objects would produce '[object Object]'.
findings.suggestion: Use a proper mapping function that handles all ExcelJS CellValue types: Date (toISOString), RichText (extract text parts), CellErrorValue, CellHyperlinkValue, etc. Or use `cell.text` which ExcelJS provides as a pre-formatted string.

findings.title: No end-to-end path connecting FolderMonitor to DocumentProcessor
findings.description: FolderMonitor and DocumentProcessor are designed to work together (monitor detects file changes, processor extracts text), but there is no code that connects them. The integration test (document-pipeline.integration.test.ts) tests DocumentProcessor -> chunkText -> VectorStore but does NOT include FolderMonitor in the pipeline. There is no walking skeleton showing the full file-watch -> extract -> chunk -> store flow.
findings.suggestion: Either create a DocumentPipeline orchestrator that wires FolderMonitor.onChange to DocumentProcessor.extractText -> chunkText -> VectorStore.insert, or extend the integration test to include FolderMonitor triggering the pipeline on file creation.

findings.title: FolderMonitor onChange callback errors lack event context
findings.description: The onChange callback is wrapped in its own try/catch, which is an improvement. However, the error message 'FolderMonitor onChange callback failed' does not include the filePath or event type that triggered the failure. Including event context makes debugging significantly easier when processing many files.
findings.suggestion: Include event context in the error message: `new Error(`FolderMonitor onChange callback failed for ${event.path} (${event.type})`, { cause: callbackError })`

findings.title: FolderMonitor copies watchPaths by reference, not by value
findings.code_snippet: this.watchPaths = options.watchPaths
findings.description: Encapsulation: 5/10. The constructor stores a direct reference to the caller's watchPaths array. If the caller mutates the array after construction (e.g., push/pop), the FolderMonitor's internal state changes silently. Combined with FolderMonitorOptions.watchPaths lacking a readonly modifier, this is a classic shared-mutable-state encapsulation leak.
findings.suggestion: Copy the array in the constructor: `this.watchPaths = [...options.watchPaths]`. Also add `readonly` to the FolderMonitorOptions.watchPaths type: `readonly watchPaths: readonly string[]`.

findings.title: Unsafe `as Record<string, unknown>` cast on pdfjs-dist text items
findings.description: Encapsulation: 6/10, Expression: 5/10, Usefulness: 6/10, Enforcement: 5/10 (Aggregate: 5.5). The pdfjs-dist TextContent.items array contains a union type (TextItem | TextMarkedContent). The code uses `'str' in item` as a discriminator but then double-casts through Record<string, unknown> instead of narrowing to TextItem. The `in` check already narrows the type in TypeScript; the `as` casts are redundant and bypass type safety.
findings.suggestion: Use proper type narrowing: `import type { TextItem } from 'pdfjs-dist'`, then check `if ('str' in item) { return (item as TextItem).str }` -- or better, define a type guard `function isTextItem(item: unknown): item is TextItem`.

findings.title: Unsafe `as` cast on exceljs row.values without type guard
findings.code_snippet: const cellValues = row.values as (string | number | boolean | null | undefined)[]
findings.description: Encapsulation: 6/10, Expression: 5/10, Usefulness: 6/10, Enforcement: 4/10 (Aggregate: 5.25). exceljs `row.values` returns `CellValue[]` which includes Date, CellErrorValue, CellRichTextValue, CellHyperlinkValue, CellFormulaValue, and CellSharedFormulaValue -- not just primitives. Casting to `(string | number | boolean | null | undefined)[]` silently discards these complex types.
findings.suggestion: Define a helper that handles the full CellValue union: extract the .result for formulas, format Dates explicitly, and convert rich text to plain string. At minimum, use `String(v)` on the original CellValue type without the narrowing cast.

findings.title: FolderMonitorOptions fields lack readonly, inconsistent with output types
findings.description: The new output types (DocumentExtractionResult, TextChunk, FileChangeEvent) correctly use readonly on all fields. However, FolderMonitorOptions has mutable watchPaths (string[]) and extensions (string[]) arrays. Since these are configuration inputs consumed by the constructor, they should be `readonly string[]` to prevent accidental mutation and to signal intent.
findings.suggestion: Change to: `readonly watchPaths: readonly string[]` and `readonly extensions?: readonly string[]`. The callback fields (onChange, onError) are fine as-is since functions are not mutated.

findings.title: Watcher callback in start() has 4 levels of nesting
findings.description: The start() method reaches 4 levels of nesting: for-loop > try > subscribe callback > for-loop over events > if extension check > try/catch around onChange. This makes the control flow difficult to follow at a glance.
findings.suggestion: Extract the watcher callback into a named private method `handleWatcherEvents(dir, err, events)`. This reduces nesting in start() from 4 to 2 levels and gives the event-handling logic a descriptive name. The extracted method is independently testable.

findings.title: Duplicated type assertion for PDF text item extraction
findings.description: The expression casts `item` to `Record<string, unknown>` twice in succession -- once for the type guard and once for the value access. After the `'str' in item` narrowing, TypeScript knows item has a `str` property. The double cast plus verbose conditional can be simplified.
findings.suggestion: Simplify to: `.map(item => ('str' in item ? String((item as { str: unknown }).str) : ''))` or use a reusable type guard function `hasStr(item): item is { str: string }`.

findings.title: FolderMonitor: delete event type not tested
findings.description: The FileChangeEvent type defines three event types: 'create', 'update', and 'delete'. The folder-monitor test suite covers create and update, but no test verifies that file deletion events are captured and reported correctly.
findings.suggestion: Add a test case that: (1) starts the monitor, (2) creates a file, (3) waits for the create event, (4) deletes the file, (5) waits for a delete event, (6) verifies event.type === 'delete' and event.filePath matches.

findings.title: FolderMonitor: onError callback never verified for watcher errors
findings.description: The FolderMonitor accepts an onError callback for watcher errors, but no test verifies this callback is invoked when an error occurs. Several tests pass `onError: () => {}` (silent swallow). No test covers the asynchronous error path when the onChange callback throws.
findings.suggestion: Add tests for: (1) FolderMonitor with an onChange that throws -- verify onError receives a wrapped error. (2) Start monitoring a directory, then delete the directory while monitoring -- verify onError is called.

findings.title: DocumentProcessor: multi-sheet XLSX and pageCount not verified
findings.description: The XLSX test only covers a single-sheet workbook. The sheet iteration logic, the double-newline separator between sheets, and the pageCount for multi-sheet workbooks are all untested.
findings.suggestion: Add tests for: (1) a workbook with 2+ sheets, (2) a workbook with an empty sheet, (3) a cell with a date or formula value.

findings.title: New output types use readonly consistently -- improved over prior types
findings.description: DocumentExtractionResult, TextChunk, and FileChangeEvent all use readonly on every field. DocumentType is a proper 4-value literal union. FileChangeEvent.type is a 3-value literal union. This is the best readonly discipline seen in this codebase and should be the template for future types.
findings.suggestion: Consider applying this same readonly pattern retroactively to older types in types.ts (ActivityEvent, ActivityContext, etc.) which currently lack readonly modifiers.

findings.title: Error messages expose full file system paths
findings.code_snippet: throw new Error(`Unsupported document type: ${extname(filePath)} (file: ${filePath})`)
findings.description: Error messages include the full absolute file path. While this is useful for debugging, if these errors propagate to a UI or API response, they could leak internal directory structure information.
findings.suggestion: Consider using a typed error class (e.g., UnsupportedDocumentTypeError) with the path stored as a property rather than embedded in the message string, allowing callers to decide what to expose.

findings.title: DocumentProcessor.extractText could use Result pattern for expected failures
findings.description: extractText throws for expected operational errors (unsupported type, corrupt file, missing file). Since file processing failures are expected in a folder monitor pipeline, a Result/Either pattern would let callers handle these as normal control flow without try/catch.
findings.suggestion: Consider returning `Promise<Result<DocumentExtractionResult, DocumentProcessorError>>` where DocumentProcessorError distinguishes UnsupportedType, CorruptFile, FileNotFound, and DependencyMissing.

findings.title: Redundant comment restates obvious loop-exit condition
findings.description: The comment 'If this chunk reached the end of text, stop' directly restates what the code does. The condition `end >= text.length` followed by `break` is self-explanatory.
findings.suggestion: Remove the comment. The code is self-documenting.

findings.title: Variable 'textParts' used for two different semantics
findings.description: Both extractPdf and extractXlsx use the variable name 'textParts' but with different semantic granularity (pages vs. sheets). Naming them 'pageTexts' and 'sheetTexts' respectively would be more self-documenting.
findings.suggestion: Rename to 'pageTexts' in extractPdf and 'sheetTexts' in extractXlsx.

findings.title: Test inline comment restates the next line of code
findings.code_snippet:
// Create a minimal valid PDF with embedded text "Hello PDF"
const pdfContent = createMinimalPdf('Hello PDF')
findings.description: The comment restates what the function call `createMinimalPdf('Hello PDF')` already communicates.
findings.suggestion: Remove the comment or consolidate test steps into a higher-level comment describing the test's intent.

positive_observations: text-chunker is a clean pure function (Functional Core) with thorough input validation and clear boundary conditions -- exemplary at 56 lines with cyclomatic complexity ~4
FolderMonitor properly handles errors in onChange callbacks by wrapping in try/catch and forwarding to onError, and requires onError (not optional) -- avoids the silent-swallow pattern
FolderMonitor.start() is idempotent and stop() gracefully handles individual unsubscribe failures
Types are well-documented with JSDoc and use readonly properties for immutable result objects -- best readonly discipline in this codebase
Integration test (document-pipeline) uses real LanceDB with temp directories -- correct pattern per project rules, with proper Test Double rationale comment
DocumentProcessor uses dynamic import for heavy dependencies (pdfjs-dist, mammoth, exceljs), reducing startup cost
No mock violations detected: zero usage of vi.fn(), vi.mock(), vi.spyOn(), InMemoryRepository, MockXxx, or FakeXxx patterns across all test files
document-processor.test.ts creates real files in all formats (hand-built PDF, DOCX via JSZip, XLSX via ExcelJS) rather than using fixture files or mocks
All JSDoc comments on exported classes and functions are factually accurate, with correct Imperative Shell / Pure function architecture annotations
The pdfjs-dist legacy build import comment is an excellent 'why' comment explaining a non-obvious technical decision
No forbidden patterns (TODO, FIXME, HACK, XXX, PLACEHOLDER, TEMP) found in any reviewed files
Error wrapping uses `new Error(msg, { cause })` consistently for error chain preservation
Extension normalization in FolderMonitor constructor handles both '.txt' and 'txt' input gracefully
All source files stay well under the 200-line threshold with clear single-responsibility
